---
title: "Geometry Ch2"
format:
  html: default
#    number-sections: true
#  pdf:
#    number-sections: true
# thm lem cor prp cnj def exm exr
---

```{python}
from helper import *
```

## Angles intro
An <i>angle</i> is formed when 2 rays share an origin, or 2 segments share an endpoint. The common origin or endpoint is the <i>vertex</i>. The 2 rays or segments are the <i>sides</i>.
Below, the angle formed by  $\overline{BF}$ and  $\overline{DF}$ is denoted $\angle BFD$ or $\angle DFB$, where the middle letter should denote the vertex. We can simply write $\angle F$ since it is unambiguous which angle it refers to, but $\angle E$ is ambiguous, so we should use 3 letters when E is the vertex.

::: {#exr-2.1}

Below, what are all the possible angles that have point E as a vertex? (Even before listing them, can we figure out how many there are?)

:::


```{python}
#| echo: false
a = (200,250)
b = (600,250)
c = (300,350)
d = (500,150)
e = (400,250)
img = np.ones((500, 800, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
cv2.line(img, a, b, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, c, d, color, thickness=2)
for pt in [a, c]:
    cv2.circle(img, pt, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
cv2.putText(img, "A", (a[0] - 25, a[1] + 5), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "B", (b[0] - 10, b[1] + 25), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "C", (c[0] - 25, c[1] + 5), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "D", (d[0] - 10, d[1] - 15), font, 0.6, color, 2)
img0=img.copy()
f = (700,150)
cv2.line(img, b, f, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, d, f, color, thickness=2)
cv2.putText(img, "E", (e[0] - 20, e[1] - 15), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "F", (f[0] + 15, f[1] + 5), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img)
```

## Measuring Angles
Use a <i>protractor</i> (shaped like a <i>semicircle</i>, ie half of a circle) to measure angles with an eye toward comparing them. The semicircle is split into 180 equal <i>degrees</i>, abbreviated ${}^\circ$. Hence, the whole circle is $360^\circ$. The Babylonians used base 60 and probably liked 360 since it has many factors.
We use a 'm' before the angle to denote its measure, eg $m \angle BED=45^\circ$ above (the 'm' is commonly omitted).
If an angle has measure $m$, it is a <i>right angle</i>, and its sides are <i>perpendicular</i>, often abbreviated $\perp$. It is <i>acute</i> if $m<90^\circ$, and <i>obtuse</i> if $90^\circ <m<180^\circ$.
Below, $\angle ABD$ is a right angle, and $\overline{BF}\perp \overline{DF}$. Note the special marking to denote a right angle.
Above, $\angle BED$ is acute, while $\angle AED$ is obtuse. We can also technically measure $\angle BDF$ starting at $\overline{BF}$ and going clockwise to $\overline{DF}$. A <i>reflex angle</i> has measure $>180^\circ$. We rarely use them since we can equivalently take their measure to be $\leq 180^\circ$.<br>
Let O be a circle's center, A,B be 2 points on $\odot$. If $\angle AOB$ cuts out proportion $p$ of the circle, then $m \angle AOB=p*360^\circ$. Common proportions are $p=1/3,1/4,1/6,1/8,1/12$, corresponding to angle measures $120^\circ, 90^\circ, 60^\circ, 45^\circ, 30^\circ$.<br>
<i>Adjacent angles</i> share a side, and form a 'bigger' angle whose measure is the sum of them. Below, $m \angle ABD=m \angle ABC+m \angle CBD$. We frequently need to find 1 of these angles' measure given the other 2.
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-2.2
# fig-cap: 'figure caption'
a = (80,110)
b = (200,350)
c = (320,110)
d = (440,230)
img = np.ones((400, 550, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
cv2.line(img, a, b, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, c, b, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, d, (140,380), color, thickness=2)
perp_marker=(170,340) #next 3 lines drae the marker for right angles
draw_perp(img, perp_marker, a, b)
draw_perp(img, perp_marker, (140,380), b)
for pt in [a, c, d]:
    cv2.circle(img, pt, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
cv2.putText(img, "A", (a[0] - 10, a[1] - 15), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "B", (b[0] - 10, b[1] + 25), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "C", (c[0] - 10, c[1] - 15), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "D", (d[0] + 15, d[1] + 5), font, 0.6, color, 2)
img1=img.copy()
show_geometry_image(img)
```

## Straight and Vertical angles
Unless otherwise stated, this whole section uses this figure
```{python}
#| echo: false
e = (400,250)
img=img0.copy()
# Center, axes (radius_x, radius_y), angle of rotation, start_angle, end_angle
cv2.ellipse(img, e, (30, 30), 0, 135, 180, color, thickness=1)  # AEC
cv2.ellipse(img, e, (30, 30), 0, 315, 360, color, thickness=1)  # BED
cv2.ellipse(img, e, (30, 30), 0, 180, 315, color, thickness=1)  # AED
cv2.line(img, (400 - 11, 250 - 26), (400 - 15, 250 - 35), color, thickness=1)  # AED tick
cv2.ellipse(img, e, (30, 30), 0, 0, 135, color, thickness=1)  # BEC
cv2.line(img, (400 + 11, 250 + 26), (400 + 15, 250 + 35), color, thickness=1)  # BEC tick
cv2.putText(img, "E", (e[0] - 35, e[1] - 35), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img)
```
A single line (segment) forms a <i>straight angle</i> that measures $180^\circ$. 2 angles whose measures add to $180^\circ$ are <i>supplementary</i>, and <i>complementary</i> if they add to $90^\circ$. <i>Vertical angles</i> are formed at opposite ends of 2 intersecting lines (or segments). Thus, we have 2 pairs of vertical angles at such an intersection.
Above, $\angle AEB$ and $\angle BED$ are straight angles. $\angle AED$ and $\angle DEB$ are supplementary. $\angle DEB$ and $\angle AEC$ are vertical angles.

::: {#exm-2.3}

If $m \angle DEB=45^\circ$, find $m \angle AEC$.

:::

Using the exact same logic to solve this problem we can prove a more general result. As a warning, an example is not a proof.
When problem solving, keep in mind that we usually cannot get the desired result in 1 step, and need to derive intermediate results that can be insightful in their own right.

::: {#lem-2.3}

If $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$ intersect at point E, then $m \angle DEB=m \angle AEC$. Thus, vertical angles always have the same measure.

:::

::: {.proof}

$\angle AEB$ and $\angle CED$ are both straight angles, so $180^\circ =m \angle AEB= m \angle AEC+m \angle CEB$ and $180^\circ =m \angle CED=m \angle CEB+m \angle DEB$, so $m \angle AEC+m \angle CEB=m \angle CEB+m \angle DEB$, so $m \angle AEC=m \angle DEB$.

:::

Just as with tick marks on segments, little arcs on angles mark those with equal measure.

::: {#exr-2.3}

Graph $y=3x/4$. Use a protractor to find the measure of the angle it forms with the $x$ axis? (It is not a whole number, so within $1^\circ$) Next, graph $y=4x/3$. Without using a protractor again, what is the measure of the angle it forms with the $x$ axis (hint: consider the angle it forms with the $y$ axis) and with $y=3x/4$?<br>
Consider the figure above. Prove that (no matter how exactly $\overline{DE}$ intersects with $\overline{AB}$) the 2 angles $\angle AED$ and $\angle BED$ are either both right angles, or 1 is acute and the other is obtuse.

:::

::: {#exr-2.3.2}

Below (@fig-2.2 repeated), prove that $\angle ABD$ and $\angle CBD$ are complementary.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
show_geometry_image(img1)
```

## Parallel lines
2+ lines are <i>parallel</i> if they do not intersect (line segments/ rays are parallel if their line extensions are). A <i>transversal</i> is a line/ segment that cuts across 2+ parallel lines.
Below, $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ (line $k$) and $\overleftrightarrow{BD}$ (line $m$) are parallel. We write $\overleftrightarrow{AC}\parallel \overleftrightarrow{BD}$ or line $k \parallel m$. Note the '>' marks to indicate parallel lines. They can be omitted if they clutter the diagram. $\overline{EH}$ is a traversal of lines $k$ and $m$. For convenience, we can also indicate angle measures on diagrams, eg $m \angle CAH=x$ below.
Recall the Parallel postulate (among Euclid's 5 axioms), which in this case says that there is a unique line passing through point B that is parallel to $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$.<br>
<b>Axiom</b>: Given parallel lines $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ and $\overleftrightarrow{BD}$, and a transversal $\overline{EH}$ (eg, as below), we have $m \angle HAG=m \angle ABF$.
```{python}
#| echo: false
def scale_pt(x,y):
    return (250 + int(x * 150), 500 - int(y * 150))
a = scale_pt(1, 2)
b = scale_pt(0, 0)
c = scale_pt(0, 2)
d = scale_pt(-1, 0)
e = scale_pt(-0.5,-1)
f = scale_pt(1, 0)
g = scale_pt(2, 2)
h = scale_pt(1.5, 3)
img = np.ones((700, 700, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
double_arrow_line(img, scale_pt(-1.5, 2), scale_pt(3.0, 2))
double_arrow_line(img, scale_pt(-1.5, 0), scale_pt(3.0, 0))
cv2.line(img, e, h, color, thickness=2)
# Parallel indicators (">" shapes on lines k and m), Draw a 1-pixel line segment to render only the arrowhead tip
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(9/4, 2.0), scale_pt(2.26, 2.0), color, thickness=2, tipLength=10.0)
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(9/4, 0.0), scale_pt(2.26, 0.0), color, thickness=2, tipLength=10.0)
img2=img.copy()
# Angle CAH marker (arc centered at A)
#cv2.ellipse(img, a, (25, 25), 0, 243, 270, color, thickness=1)
for pt in [c, d, e, f, g, h]:
    cv2.circle(img, pt, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
cv2.putText(img, "A", (a[0] + 15, a[1] + 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "B", (b[0] + 15, b[1] + 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "C", (c[0] - 25, c[1] - 10), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "D", (d[0] - 10, d[1] + 25), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "E", (e[0] - 10, e[1] + 25), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "F", (f[0] - 10, f[1] + 25), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "G", (g[0] - 15, g[1] - 10), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "H", (h[0] + 15, h[1] - 10), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "k", (675, 175), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "m", (675, 475), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "x", (a[0] - 10, a[1] - 10), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img)
```

::: {#lem-2.4}

Given parallel lines $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ and $\overleftrightarrow{BD}$, and a transversal $\overline{EH}$ (eg, as above), we have $m \angle HAG=m \angle ABF=m \angle EBD=m \angle BAC$, and $m \angle CAH=m \angle BAG=m \angle EBF=m \angle DBA$. Further, any angle in the 1st group is supplementary to any angle in the 2nd. The equal angle measures are marked as below.

:::

::: {.proof}

By @lem-2.3, $m \angle HAG=m \angle BAC$ and $m \angle ABF=m \angle EBD$. By the axiom, $m \angle HAG=m \angle ABF$, so all 4 angles have equal measure. The same argument gives $m \angle CAH=m \angle BAG=m \angle EBF=m \angle DBA$. To see any angle in the 1st group is supplementary to any angle in the 2nd, we consider 1 representative angle per group, eg $\angle CAH$ and $\angle BAC$ clearly sum to $180^\circ$.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
img=img2.copy()
cv2.circle(img, a, radius=25, color=color, thickness=1)
cv2.circle(img, b, radius=25, color=color, thickness=1)
cv2.line(img, (a[0] - 12, a[1] - 12), (a[0] - 24, a[1] - 24), color, thickness=1)
cv2.line(img, (a[0] + 12, a[1] + 12), (a[0] + 24, a[1] + 24), color, thickness=1)
cv2.line(img, (b[0] - 12, b[1] - 12), (b[0] - 24, b[1] - 24), color, thickness=1)
cv2.line(img, (b[0] + 12, b[1] + 12), (b[0] + 24, b[1] + 24), color, thickness=1)
show_geometry_image(img)
```
We now consider angle chasing problems, where we use information about some angles to deduce information about others. Best practices for angle chasing (and problem solving in general): mark segments of the same length and angles of the same measure in diagrams. Assign variables for unknown quantities and build equations to solve for them. To that end, 3 helpful tools so far (more to come) are supplementary angles, vertical angles, and parallel lines (so useful that we often introduce them).


::: {#exm-2.4.1}

Below, $m \angle CBD=x$, $m \angle BCD=y$, $m \angle BDF=3x-15$, and $m \angle DBE=3y+15$. Find $x,y$.<br>
Variant: $m \angle CBD=x$, $m \angle BCD=x+10$, and $m \angle DBE=x^2/5-5$, find $m \angle BDF$.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
def scale_pt(x,y):
    return (60 + int(x * 120), 500 - int(y * 150))
img = np.ones((700, 720, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
a = scale_pt(1, 2)
b = scale_pt(4, 2)
c = scale_pt(0, 0)
d = scale_pt(3, 0)
e = scale_pt(5, 2)
f = scale_pt(4, 0)
double_arrow_line(img, scale_pt(-0.5, 2), scale_pt(5.5, 2))
double_arrow_line(img, scale_pt(-0.5, 0), scale_pt(5.5, 0))
cv2.line(img, a, c, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, b, d, color, thickness=2)
# Parallel markers
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(0.5, 1), scale_pt(0.55, 1.1), color, thickness=2, tipLength=1.0)
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(3.5, 1), scale_pt(3.55, 1.1), color, thickness=2, tipLength=1.0)
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(3, 2), scale_pt(3.1, 2), color, thickness=2, tipLength=1.0)
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(1, 0), scale_pt(1.1, 0), color, thickness=2, tipLength=1.0)
img0=img.copy()
cv2.line(img, b, c, color, thickness=2)
for pt in [e, f]:
    cv2.circle(img, pt, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
for lbl in list('ABCDEF'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0] + 20, p[1] + 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img)
```
There are multiple ways to find $x,y$. Taking 2 different approaches is a good way to confirm. In fact, we have 3 equations: $3x-15+3y+15=180$, $y+x+3y+15=180$, and $x+y=3x-15$. Any 2 of them are linearly independent, and lead to $x,y$.

::: {#exm-2.4.3}

Below, $m \angle CAE=140^\circ$, $m \angle FDG=x$, and $m \angle BDE=3x$. Find $x$.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
img=img0.copy()
e = scale_pt(2, 2)
f = scale_pt(3, -1)
g = scale_pt(3.5, -1)
cv2.line(img, d, f, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, e, g, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, (400,520), (400,500), color, thickness=1) #perp marker
cv2.line(img, (400,520), (420,520), color, thickness=1)
for pt in [f, g]:
    cv2.circle(img, pt, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
for lbl in list('ABCEF'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0] - 20, p[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, 'D', (d[0] + 30, d[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, 'G', (g[0] + 20, g[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img)
```

::: {#exm-2.4.3}

Below, $m \angle BEG=130^\circ$. Find $m \angle GDC$. Hint: draw the line parallel to $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$ that passes through point $G$.
In general, for 2 pairs of parallel lines (eg, as below), prove that $m \angle ABD=m \angle ACD$, and $m \angle CAB=m \angle CDB$.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
img=img0.copy()
e = scale_pt(2, 2)
g = scale_pt(1.4, 0.8)
cv2.line(img, g, d, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, e, g, color, thickness=2)
cv2.line(img, (264,365), (240,350), color, thickness=1) #perp marker
cv2.line(img, (264,365), (252,395), color, thickness=1)
cv2.circle(img, g, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
for lbl in list('ABCEG'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0] - 20, p[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, 'D', (d[0] + 30, d[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
#cv2.putText(img, 'G', (g[0] + 20, g[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img,y_px=250)
```

::: {#exr-2.4}

If 2 line segments do not intersect, why are they not necessarily parallel?

:::

## Angles in a triangle
A <i>triangle</i> is formed when segments connect 3 points (not collinear). The segments are the triangle's <i>sides</i>, and the points are its <i>vertices</i>.
We write $\triangle ABC$ (sometimes just $ABC$) to denote the triangle below. Its vertices are $A,B,C$. Its sides are $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{AC}$, and $\overline{BC}$.

::: {#lem-2.5}

A triangle's 3 angles sum to $180^\circ$, ie $m \angle A+m \angle B+m \angle ACB=180^\circ$ below.

:::

This is among geometry's most used results. Try using it as an alternative to solving some of the previous problems (eg, in @exm-2.4.3, extend $\overline{EG}$ to $\overleftrightarrow{CD}$).

::: {.proof}

Use the parallel postulate to draw a line through point C parallel to $\overline{AB}$. Then $180^\circ =m \angle ACD+m \angle ACB+m \angle BCE$. By @lem-2.4, $m \angle ACD=m \angle A$, and $m \angle BCE=m \angle B$, so $m \angle A+m \angle ACB+m \angle B=180^\circ$ as desired.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
def scale_pt(x,y):
    return (100 + int(x * 100), 700 - int(y * 100))
img = np.ones((800, 800, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
a = scale_pt(0, 0)
b = scale_pt(6, 0)
c = scale_pt(4, 4)
d = scale_pt(2, 4)
e = scale_pt(6, 4)
draw_path(img, a, b, c, a)
img0=img.copy()
double_arrow_line(img, scale_pt(-0.5, 4), scale_pt(6.5, 4), thickness=1)
# Parallel markers
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(3, 0), scale_pt(3.1, 0), color, thickness=1, tipLength=2.0)
cv2.arrowedLine(img, scale_pt(3, 4), scale_pt(3.1, 4), color, thickness=1, tipLength=2.0)
#angle markers
cv2.ellipse(img,c,(25,25),0,0,63,color,thickness=1)
cv2.line(img,(c[0]+16,c[1]+12),(c[0]+24,c[1]+18), color,thickness=1)
cv2.ellipse(img,b,(25,25),0,180,243,color,thickness=1)
cv2.line(img,(b[0]-16,b[1]-12),(b[0]-24,b[1]-18), color,thickness=1)
cv2.ellipse(img,c,(25,25),0,135,180,color,thickness=1)
cv2.ellipse(img,a,(25,25),0,315,360,color,thickness=1)
for pt in [d, e]:
    cv2.circle(img, pt, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
for lbl in list('ABCDE'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0], p[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img[200:,:,:])
```

::: {#exm-2.5}

Below, $m \angle E=50^\circ$, $m \angle ECB=m \angle ADC=3x$, and $m \angle BCD=x$. Find $x$.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
img=img0.copy()
d = scale_pt(2, 0)
e = scale_pt(6, 6)
draw_path(img,d,c,e,scale_pt(6,-0.5))
cv2.line(img, (680,720), (680,700), color, thickness=1) #perp marker
cv2.line(img, (680,720), (700,720), color, thickness=1)
for lbl in list('ABDE'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0] + 20, p[1] + 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, 'C', (c[0], c[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img)
```

::: {#exr-2.5}

If a triangle has a right angle, prove the other 2 are complementary.<br>
In a triangle, 1 angle has twice the measure of a 2nd, and the 3rd is $54^\circ$. What is the smallest angle's measure?

:::

## Exterior angles
2 sides of a triangle form an <i>interior angle</i>. Extending 1 of these sides past the vertex, we get an <i>exterior angle</i>. Below, $\angle ACB$ is an interior angle of $\triangle ABC$, and $\angle ECB$ is the corresponding exterior angle.

::: {#lem-2.6}

In any triangle, an interior angle and the corresponding exterior angle are supplementary. The exterior angle's measure is the sum of the measures of the other 2 interior angles (called the remote interior angles).
Concretely (eg as below), $\angle ACB$ and $\angle ECB$ are supplementary, and $m \angle ECB=m \angle A+ \angle B$.

:::

::: {.proof}

Clearly $m \angle ACB+m \angle ECB=180^\circ$. By @lem-2.5, $m \angle ACB+m \angle A+m \angle B=180^\circ$. Thus, $m \angle ECB=m \angle A+ \angle B$.

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
img=img0.copy()
e = scale_pt(6, 6)
cv2.line(img,c,e,color,thickness=2)
cv2.circle(img, e, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
for lbl in list('ABCE'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0], p[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_geometry_image(img)
```

::: {#exr-2.6}

Given an interior angle of a triangle, either of its sides can be extended, so any interior angle has 2 exterior angles. Prove that both of these exterior angles have the same measure.<br>
Find the sum of the measures of a triangle's 3 exterior angles.

:::

## Proving lines are parallel

The <i>converse</i> of a statement 'if A then B' is the statement 'if B then A.' If the original statement is true, the converse is not necessarily true. So, even if it is true, it must be proven separately.
Eg, given the statement 'if a pet is a cat, then it has 4 legs' (assume no missing limbs), its converse 'if a pet has 4 legs, then it is a cat' is false (could be a dog). We now show the converse to @lem-2.4 is true.

::: {#lem-2.7}

Below (left panel), if either $w=y$, $w=z$, or $x=y$, then $\overleftrightarrow{AB}\parallel \overleftrightarrow{CD}$.

:::

::: {.proof}

Assume $x=y$, and for contradiction that $\overleftrightarrow{AB}$ intersects $\overleftrightarrow{CD}, say at point $E$, eg as below, right panel. (A full proof must also consider the case $w=y$ and $w=z$, and if point $E$ is to the right of $D$, ie 5 other cases).
Then by @lem-2.6, $x=y+m \angle E$. Since $x=y$, this means $m \angle E=0^\circ$. But this contradicts the diagram.

:::

All our proofs before this have been direct proofs. Here we used a <i>proof by contradiction</i>, where, under the given conditions, assuming the opposite of the desired result leads to a contradiction (something impossible).

```{python}
#| echo: false
img=img2.copy()
a=(250,200)
b=(550,200)
c=(100,500)
d=(400,500)
for lbl in list('ABCD'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.circle(img, p, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0], p[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "w", (380, 180), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "x", (420, 220), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "y", (230, 480), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img, "z", (270, 520), font, 0.6, color, 2)
img3 = np.ones((700, 700, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
a=(550,275)
b=(650,225)
c=(425,500)
d=(600,500)
e=(100,500)
double_arrow_line(img3, (50, 525), (680, 210))
double_arrow_line(img3, (50, 500), (680, 500))
double_arrow_line(img3, (425, 600), (650, 150))
for lbl in list('ABCD'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.circle(img3, p, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
    cv2.putText(img3, lbl, (p[0], p[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img3, 'E', (e[0], e[1] - 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img3, "w", (575, 225), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img3, "x", (625, 275), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img3, "y", (450, 475), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.putText(img3, "z", (500, 525), font, 0.6, color, 2)
show_many_images(img,img3,nrows=1)
```

::: {#exr-2.7}

Below, if $m \angle FAB=121^\circ$ and $m \angle ABC=m \angle CDE=29^\circ$, is $\angle BCE$ a right angle?

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
def scale_pt(x,y):
    return (300 + int(x * 100), 300 - int(y * 100))
img = np.ones((600, 600, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
a=(200,300)
b=(300,100)
c=(300,300)
d=(300,500)
e=(400,300)
f=(100,300)
double_arrow_line(img, (50, 300), (550, 300))
double_arrow_line(img, (300, 50), (300, 550))
double_arrow_line(img, (100, 500), (325, 50))
double_arrow_line(img, (500, 100), (275, 550))
for lbl in list('ABCDEF'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img, lbl, (p[0]+10, p[1] + 20), font, 0.6, color, 2)
cv2.circle(img, f, radius=4, color=color, thickness=-1)
cv2.ellipse(img, b, (30, 30), 0, 90, 117, color, thickness=1)
cv2.ellipse(img, d, (30, 30), 0, 270, 297, color, thickness=1)
show_geometry_image(img)
```

## More exercises

::: {#exr-2.8.1}

The hour and minute hand on a clock meet at 12:00 (noon or midnight). Find another time of day where they meet. (It will not be at a whole number of minutes or seconds, round to the nearest)

:::

::: {#exr-2.8.2}

Given that $\overleftrightarrow{PQ}\parallel \overleftrightarrow{RS}$, $\overleftrightarrow{PQ}\perp \overleftrightarrow{TV}$, and $\overleftrightarrow{TV}$ intersects $\overleftrightarrow{PQ}$ at point $X$ and $\overleftrightarrow{RS}$ at point $Y$. Find $m \angle RYX$.

:::

::: {#exr-2.8.3}

Below, is it possible for $m \angle CDE=x$, $m \angle DEB=x+2y$, and $m \angle EBA=y$?

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
img = np.ones((600, 600, 3), dtype=np.uint8) * 255
a=(200,500)
b=(450,500)
c=(200,100)
d=(450,100)
e=(300,300)
draw_path(img,a,b,e,d,c)
for p in [a,c]:
    cv2.circle(img,p,radius=4,color=color,thickness=1)
for lbl in list('ABCD'):
    p=globals()[lbl.lower()]
    cv2.putText(img,lbl,(p[0],p[1]+20),font,0.6,color,2)
cv2.putText(img,'E',(e[0]-20,e[1]),font,0.6,color,2)
cv2.arrowedLine(img, (300, 500), (310, 500), color, thickness=2, tipLength=1.0)
cv2.arrowedLine(img, (300, 100), (310, 100), color, thickness=2, tipLength=1.0)
show_geometry_image(img)
```

::: {#exr-2.8.4}

Given a triangle with angle measures $x-2y$, $3x+5y$, and $5x-3y$, find $x$. If 1 of the angles is $10^\circ$, find all possible values of $y$.

:::

::: {#exr-2.8.5}

$\overline{AD}$, $\overline{BE}$, and $\overline{CF}$ are concurrent at point $O$. The points are arranged clockwise around $O$ in order: A B C D E F. If $m \angle COD=m \angle DOE$, and the ratio of $m \angle COB$ and $m \angle BOF$ is $7:2$, find $m \angle COD$.

:::
